question.text: A commodity user (processor) who needs to buy raw materials in the future should adopt:

answer.text: A long hedge — buy futures to lock in the purchase price
A short hedge — sell futures to lock in the purchase price
No hedge — wait for prices to fall naturally
A spread trade between two different commodities

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A processor needs to BUY raw materials (naturally short). A long hedge (buying futures) locks in the purchase price. If prices rise, futures profit offsets higher spot cost. **The Trap:** Short hedge is for SELLERS (producers). No hedge leaves the processor exposed. Spread trades don't directly hedge purchase costs. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII covers long hedging for portfolio buyers. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Processors should hedge 3-6 months of raw material requirements using rolling futures — this provides production cost certainty for business planning.
